executiveSummary:
# Executive Summary: Medicaid Program Performance Analysis

**Enrollment and Financial Trends**

National Medicaid enrollment stands at 75.3 million as of January 2026, with virtually all major states experiencing enrollment declines as the post-pandemic unwinding continues. Texas enrollment decreased 3.1% year-over-year to 4.0 million, outperforming the steepest declines seen in Michigan (-7.6%) and California and Illinois (both -6.2%), while tracking similarly to Pennsylvania's 3.1% reduction. North Carolina stands as a notable outlier with modest growth of 0.5%. From a spending perspective, fiscal year 2024 data shows total national expenditures of $215.9 billion with a per-enrollee average of $2,869. Texas spending totaled $12.0 billion at $2,988 per enrollee, positioning the state slightly above the national average but significantly below high-cost states like Pennsylvania ($4,377 per enrollee) and Massachusetts ($4,201 per enrollee). The federal government's 66% cost share ($142.0 billion of total spending) remains consistent with historical patterns.

**Quality Performance and Program Structure**

Quality measure performance reveals substantial variation across states, with a national average of 65.6 out of 100 points and only 10 states achieving high performance scores above 75. Texas scored 62 points, ranking 30th nationally in the moderate performance tier, falling short of leading states like Massachusetts (81) and Minnesota (80) but substantially outperforming bottom-tier states such as Mississippi (48) and Oklahoma (51). The state's managed care penetration rate of 93.3% significantly exceeds the 83.2% national average, with nearly 3.9 million enrollees in managed care arrangements. Recent federal regulatory activity indicates heightened scrutiny of state directed payments and provider enrollment moratoria, while Texas procurement activity suggests ongoing contract management and potential program adjustments that warrant monitoring for operational and policy implications.
texasSpotlight:
## Texas Medicaid Program Performance Analysis

Texas operates the nation's third-largest Medicaid program with 4.03 million enrollees, representing a substantial commitment to serving low-income populations despite remaining a non-expansion state. The program demonstrates mixed performance across key metrics. On the positive side, Texas has embraced managed care more extensively than most states, with 93.3% penetration compared to the 83.2% national average, positioning the state well for coordinated care delivery and cost management. Per-enrollee spending of $2,988 sits just above the national average of $2,869 but remains competitive among large states—significantly lower than expansion states like New York ($3,431) and Pennsylvania ($4,377), though higher than fellow non-expansion state Florida ($2,031). The state's enrollment decline of 3.1% year-over-year aligns closely with the national trend as states complete post-pandemic eligibility redeterminations.

However, Texas faces notable quality challenges that warrant immediate attention. The state's quality score of 62/100 ranks 30th nationally and falls into the "moderate" tier—a concerning position for a state serving over 4 million vulnerable residents. This performance lags behind regional peers like North Carolina, which has achieved better outcomes while maintaining lower per-enrollee costs ($2,616). Among large non-expansion states, Texas appears to be underperforming relative to its resources and infrastructure investments. The concentration of federal regulatory activity around provider enrollment moratoria and state-directed payments suggests increased federal scrutiny of program integrity. Moving forward, Texas should leverage its strong managed care platform to drive quality improvements while maintaining cost discipline, particularly as the state continues to serve a large uninsured population that could benefit from potential future expansion considerations.

alerts.title: Pennsylvania Per-Enrollee Spending 53% Above National Average
alerts.description: Pennsylvania's per-enrollee spending of $4,377 is significantly higher than the national average of $2,869, indicating potential cost inefficiencies or program issues.

alerts.title: Mississippi Quality Score Critical - 33% Below National Avg
alerts.description: Mississippi scored 48/100 on quality measures, ranking last nationally and falling 27% below the national average of 65.6.

alerts.title: Four Southern States in Bottom Quality Performers
alerts.description: Arkansas, South Carolina, Alabama, and Oklahoma all scored below 55 on quality measures, indicating regional quality concerns in the Southeast.

alerts.title: Florida Low Per-Enrollee Spending Raises Access Concerns
alerts.description: Florida's per-enrollee spending of $2,031 is 29% below the national average, potentially indicating inadequate service provision despite high enrollment.

alerts.title: Massachusetts Achieves High Quality Despite High Spending
alerts.description: Massachusetts leads quality scores at 81/100 while spending $4,201 per enrollee, demonstrating effective high-investment care delivery.

executiveInsights.title: TX Medicaid quality score at risk tier with 20-state gap to top performers
executiveInsights.summary: Texas ranks #30 with a 62/100 quality score, 19 points below the top tier threshold of 75+ achieved by 10 states. Massachusetts leads at 81/100, showing a 19-point performance gap.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Quality performance directly impacts contract renewals and CMS oversight intensity. Poor scores can trigger federal intervention and affect future contract terms.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag quality improvement initiatives to HHSC leadership

executiveInsights.title: TX spending $120 above national average per enrollee at $2,988
executiveInsights.summary: Texas spends $2,988 per enrollee compared to the national average of $2,869, representing $120 excess per member. With 4M+ enrollees, this suggests potential cost optimization opportunities.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Higher per-member costs indicate potential inefficiencies that could be addressed through better managed care strategies or administrative improvements, directly impacting contract value.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review cost benchmarking analysis with HHSC finance team

executiveInsights.title: Nationwide enrollment moratorium signals major regulatory shift
executiveInsights.summary: CMS announced nationwide temporary moratoriums on Medicare, Medicaid, and CHIP enrollment in May 2026. Two separate Federal Register entries indicate significant program changes ahead.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Enrollment freezes could fundamentally alter Texas Medicaid operations and contract scope, requiring immediate strategic adjustments and stakeholder communication.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag moratorium implications to HHSC immediately

executiveInsights.title: TX managed care penetration 10 points above national at 93.3%
executiveInsights.summary: Texas achieves 93.3% managed care penetration versus 83.2% nationally, with 3.86M managed care enrollees. This represents strong operational efficiency in care coordination.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: High managed care penetration demonstrates successful program implementation and positions Texas favorably for value-based care initiatives and federal demonstrations.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review managed care success metrics for replication opportunities

executiveInsights.title: CMS updating state directed payment rules affecting managed care
executiveInsights.summary: New Federal Register entry addresses Medicaid managed care state directed payments and fee-for-service targeted practitioner regulations, effective 2026.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Payment methodology changes could impact Texas's managed care contracts and provider reimbursement structures, requiring contract modifications and compliance updates.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review payment rule changes with HHSC managed care team

riskOpportunity.label: Managed care contract renewal
riskOpportunity.description: Texas's 93.3% managed care penetration creates significant contract renewal opportunities as existing arrangements come up for recompete.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Proactively engage HHSC stakeholders and position Accenture's managed care transformation capabilities.

riskOpportunity.label: Quality performance gap
riskOpportunity.description: Texas ranks #30 with a 62/100 quality score, presenting both improvement opportunities and potential regulatory scrutiny risks.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop comprehensive quality improvement strategy targeting specific CMS measures where Texas underperforms.

riskOpportunity.label: Enrollment decline pressure
riskOpportunity.description: Texas's -3.1% enrollment decline creates budget pressures and potential scope reductions for existing contracts.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Propose cost optimization initiatives and demonstrate value through improved per-enrollee outcomes.

riskOpportunity.label: Federal payment regulation changes
riskOpportunity.description: New CMS regulations on state directed payments and managed care could create compliance requirements and modernization needs.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Establish regulatory compliance practice and offer proactive guidance to HHSC on upcoming federal requirements.

riskOpportunity.label: HHS OIG oversight intensity
riskOpportunity.description: Active federal oversight creates compliance risks but also opportunities to demonstrate governance capabilities and risk mitigation.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Strengthen compliance monitoring frameworks and position Accenture as trusted advisor for audit readiness.

riskOpportunity.label: Texas procurement pipeline access
riskOpportunity.description: Active HHSC procurement opportunities through multiple portals create competitive positioning challenges and new business prospects.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Establish systematic monitoring of Texas ESBD and HHSC portals with dedicated bid response capabilities.

riskOpportunity.label: Per-enrollee cost efficiency
riskOpportunity.description: Texas's $2,988 per-enrollee spending is above national average but below peer states, creating optimization pressure and opportunity.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop benchmarking analysis and propose targeted cost reduction initiatives while maintaining quality outcomes.

riskOpportunity.label: Provider enrollment moratorium impact
riskOpportunity.description: Federal temporary moratorium on provider enrollments could disrupt network adequacy and create system modernization needs.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Assess network adequacy implications and propose alternative provider engagement strategies for HHSC.

pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas Medicaid serves 4,032,864 enrollees as of January 2026.

pulseMetrics.label: Per Enrollee Spend
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas spends $2,988 per Medicaid enrollee annually in fiscal year 2024.

pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas has 93.3% managed care penetration with 3,863,808 enrollees in managed care.

pulseMetrics.label: Quality Score
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas ranks #30 of 50 states with a moderate quality score of 62 out of 100.

pulseMetrics.label: Active Procurement Items
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas has 2 active procurement opportunities tracked across HHSC and ESBD portals.

pulseMetrics.label: OIG/Regulatory Signals
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas has 2 active OIG oversight items in the federal monitoring portfolio.

pulseMetrics.label: Next Milestone
pulseMetrics.tooltip: No specific milestone dates available in current data sources.
